steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what essays "Concerning Dangers from Dissensions between the States" (No. 6) and "The Senate" (No. 62) have in common. Both facts must be confirmed by the passage — one false detail eliminates a choice entirely.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — They were both written by one of the three authors of the Federalist Papers, but which of the authors wrote either is not entirely clear.: False for No. 6 — the passage states No. 6 "was surely penned by Hamilton," so its authorship is certain, not unclear. ✗
  • Choice B — They were both published sometime during 1787 or 1788.: The passage states all 85 Federalist Papers were published in 1787–88. Both essays are part of that collection, so both share that publication window. ✓
  • Choice C — They were both collaborative efforts between Alexander Hamilton and James Madison.: No. 6 was written solely by Hamilton; No. 62 was written by either Hamilton or Madison — neither is described as a collaboration. ✗
  • Choice D — They were both published in the Daily Advertiser.: The passage says publication was divided "among the Daily Advertiser and other New York newspapers" — it doesn't confirm both essays appeared in that specific paper. ✗
That is the correct answer.
